segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Listen to a talk in a history class.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: The age of exploration extended from the 15th century to the 17th century, but long before this, seafarers were venturing into unfamiliar waters using remarkably sophisticated techniques.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: One notable example is Pytheas of Massalia, a Greek explorer from the 4th century BCE.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: He sailed from the Mediterranean to the northern reaches of Europe, possibly reaching Britain and even Iceland.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Without modern instruments, Pytheas relied on coastal landmarks, the position of the sun and stars, and careful observation of ocean tides and daylight patterns.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: His journey demonstrated that long-distance navigation was possible through keen environmental awareness and methodical planning.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Centuries later, European explorers expanded on these early achievements with the help of new technologies.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Among the most transformative was the magnetic compass, which used a magnetized needle to indicate direction.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Originally developed in China and later adopted in Europe, the compass allowed sailors to maintain a heading even when celestial bodies were obscured by clouds or fog.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: This innovation enabled mariners to venture into open seas with greater confidence and precision.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Historians note that the compass didn't just improve navigation; it transformed it.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: It allowed explorers like Vasco da Gama and Ferdinand Magellan to chart new maritime routes across vast oceans, connecting continents and reshaping global trade.

segments.speaker: Professor
segments.text: Combined with other tools like the astrolabe and improved ship designs, the compass helped usher in the age of exploration.
